Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense signaling; required for local resistance and systemic acquired resistance (SAR)
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Expression of nahG reduces salicylic acid levels and abolishes establishment of SAR and hypersensitive resistance in tobacco
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: transgenic expression; metabolite depletion; disease resistance assays; grafting experiments
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. nahG encodes salicylate hydroxylase that converts salicylic acid to catechol. 2. Transgenic tobacco expressing nahG had greatly reduced salicylic acid levels. 3. These plants failed to establish SAR and lost hypersensitive resistance to avirulent challenge, demonstrating the importance of salicylic acid in defense signaling.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Used as a heterologous metabolic engineering tool in a review context; grafting experiments indicated salicylic acid is important but not itself the systemic mobile SAR signal.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of fungal PDA only marginally impaired virulence, questioning whether pisatin alone is sufficient for resistance
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: gene cloning; homologous recombination gene disruption; virulence assays
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PDA encodes pisatin demethylase involved in detoxification of the pea phytoalexin pisatin by the pathogen. 2. Classical genetics linked virulence with pisatin detoxification ability. 3. Gene disruption caused only marginal virulence reduction, suggesting pisatin alone is not sufficient to explain resistance.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Pathogen gene discussed in relation to host phenylpropanoid function; included because it directly determines metabolism of a nutrient-related defense phenylpropanoid.

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: stress-induced phenylpropanoids; salicylic acid; chlorogenic acid; lignin-related phenolics
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: entry step from phenylalanine to cinnamate
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Deaminates L-phenylalanine to form cinnamic acid, linking shikimate-derived primary metabolism to phenylpropanoid metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: upstream; branch point; committed entry step
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: PAL activity and expression determine flux into multiple stress-induced phenylpropanoids; suppression reduces constitutive phenolics and compromises defense and SAR
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: enzyme purification; transcript analysis; sense suppression in transgenic tobacco; inhibitor studies; mutant and localization studies
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PAL is the branch-point enzyme between primary shikimate metabolism and secondary phenylpropanoid metabolism. 2. PAL transcripts are rapidly induced by stress and coordinated with CHS in several systems. 3. PAL product cinnamic acid exerts feedback inhibition on PAL transcription/activity. 4. Sense-suppressed tobacco with reduced PAL expression showed reduced constitutive phenolics and impaired defense/SAR.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: UV protection, antimicrobial defense, wound barrier formation, signaling
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Multiple isoforms exist; heterotetramer formation and post-translational regulation may affect metabolic specialization. TAL activity in grasses may derive from PAL with relaxed specificity.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses p-coumaroyl-CoA with three molecules of malonyl-CoA to form chalcone, the core precursor of many flavonoids
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control point for flavonoid branch
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: CHS controls synthesis of many stress-induced flavonoids and its loss abolishes flavonoid accumulation and increases UV sensitivity
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: mutant analysis (tt4); antisense suppression; transcript localization; transgenic pathway diversion with SS
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CHS catalyzes the first committed step of flavonoid biosynthesis. 2. CHS transcripts are rapidly induced by stress and light/UV. 3. Arabidopsis tt4 mutants lacking CHS have no detectable leaf flavonoids and are UV sensitive. 4. CHS interacts functionally with CHR to enable 5-deoxyisoflavonoid formation in legumes.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: UV protection; antimicrobial defense; pollen development
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Multiple family members occur in legumes; some substrates can be diverted to stilbene synthesis by heterologous SS.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Petunia hybrida; Lotus sp.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Isomerizes chalcone to flavanone in early flavonoid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: CHI loss reduces flavonoid accumulation and increases UV sensitivity in Arabidopsis tt5 mutants
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CHI converts chalcone to flavanone in flavonoid biosynthesis. 2. Arabidopsis tt5 mutants defective in CHI lack leaf flavonoids and are highly UV sensitive. 3. Antisense suppression of CHI did not always yield the expected phenotype in petunia and Lotus, indicating gene suppression outcomes may vary.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Unexpected weak antisense phenotypes caution against overinterpreting negative suppression results.

Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: 5-hydroxylated ferulate derivatives leading to sinapate esters
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes 5-hydroxylation in the pathway leading to sinapate esters and syringyl-type phenolics
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key determinant for sinapoyl malate accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: fah1/F5H mutants lack sinapoyl malate biosynthesis and are extremely UV sensitive
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: T-DNA tagging/gene isolation; mutant phenotyping; metabolite screening
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. F5H was isolated in Arabidopsis by genetic strategies and corresponds to the fah1 locus. 2. F5H is required for sinapoyl malate and related sinapate ester biosynthesis. 3. Homozygous fah1 mutants are extremely UV sensitive, demonstrating the protective role of sinapate esters.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Also involved in synthesis of lignin monomers and wall-esterified phenolics according to the review.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: stilbene branch competing with flavonoid branch
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses cinnamoyl/p-coumaroyl-CoA with three malonyl-CoA units to form stilbenes such as resveratrol
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Heterologous expression of grapevine SS in tobacco diverts CHS substrates to resveratrol and increases resistance to Botrytis cinerea
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: transgenic expression; metabolite production; pathogen resistance assay
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SS competes for CHS-type starter substrates to produce stilbenes. 2. Introduction of a grapevine SS gene into tobacco enabled resveratrol accumulation. 3. This metabolic diversion increased resistance to the fungal pathogen Botrytis cinerea.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful transgene for engineering stilbene phytoalexin production and enhanced fungal resistance in non-stilbene-producing crops.

Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: reduced isoflavonoid intermediate en route to pterocarpans
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces isoflavonoid intermediates in the pathway toward pterocarpan phytoalexins
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: IFR is a terminal-pathway enzyme for isoflavonoid phytoalexin production; cloned legume IFRs show authentic activity whereas tobacco IFR-like proteins do not
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: gene cloning; heterologous expression in E. coli; enzyme activity assay; transcript analysis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. IFR is specific to legume isoflavonoid biosynthesis. 2. Alfalfa and pea IFR proteins expressed in E. coli matched native enzyme specificity. 3. IFR-like tobacco proteins lacked IFR activity despite sequence similarity, showing that homology alone cannot assign function.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Promoter architecture differs from central pathway genes, consistent with branch-specific regulation.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Glycine max; Medicago sativa; Petroselinum crispum
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces the CHS-bound polyketide intermediate before cyclization, enabling formation of 5-deoxyflavonoid precursors
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: CHR enables biosynthesis of 5-deoxyisoflavonoid phytoalexins through physical/functional interaction with CHS
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: biochemical studies; co-expression analysis; E. coli expression studies; site-directed mutagenesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Legumes possess CHR in addition to CHS to produce trihydroxychalcone-derived intermediates. 2. CHR must act on the CHS-bound intermediate, implying direct CHS-CHR interaction. 3. CHR and CHS are co-induced in elicited/infected soybean and alfalfa cells.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Parsley CHS can function with CHR in vitro, suggesting interaction is not limited to specific CHS isoforms.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Rearranges the flavonoid carbon skeleton to generate isoflavone intermediates, apparently with participation of a second dehydratase activity
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: IFS establishes the isoflavonoid branch leading to multiple defense-related phenylpropanoids in legumes
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: biochemical pathway analysis; P-450 cloning strategies; yeast expression proposed/used for identification
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. IFS is the pivotal enzyme that rearranges flavonoid skeletons into isoflavonoid skeletons. 2. It is a low-abundance, unstable P-450-type activity important for legume defense metabolite synthesis. 3. The step appears to require two enzymes, with the second dehydratase not fully characterized.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Function emphasized in pathway summarization; exact catalytic system incompletely characterized in the review.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers glucose from UDP-glucose to flavonoid hydroxyl groups to form glucosides
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: required for storage/modification of target product
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: UFGT was genetically cloned using Ac tagging and represents a late flavonoid modification enzyme
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. UFGT was cloned in maize using Ac transposon tagging. 2. It functions in late glycosylation of flavonoids/anthocyanins. 3. Its cloning illustrates the usefulness of genetics for isolating phenylpropanoid pathway genes.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: O-methylates hydroxycinnamate intermediates involved in lignin monomer biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Antisense suppression modifies lignin content and composition in transgenic tobacco
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. COMT participates in lignin biosynthesis. 2. Antisense suppression in tobacco altered lignin content and composition. 3. This confirmed the role of the cloned O-methyltransferase gene in phenylpropanoid metabolism.

Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: DNA binding to Box P element; transcriptional activation support
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: PAL; 4CL genes containing Box P element
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: activates/supports high transcription of PAL and related genes
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: BPF-1 is an elicitor-induced DNA-binding factor linked to activation of core phenylpropanoid genes in parsley
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: DNA ligand screening of cDNA expression library; expression analysis
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BPF-1 recognizes the conserved Box P cis-element in PAL and 4CL promoters. 2. It belongs to a novel transcription factor class lacking leucine repeat and zinc finger motifs. 3. BPF-1 transcripts are themselves induced in elicited parsley cells, consistent with a role in supporting strong PAL induction.
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: novel transcription factor without leucine repeat or zinc finger motifs
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Induced in elicited parsley cell cultures
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Contributes to coordinated induction of central phenylpropanoid flux under stress.

Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: binds functional light-responsive cis-element in CHS promoter
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: UV light; transition from dark-grown to illuminated cells
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: CPRF-1 is a UV/light-induced bZIP factor associated with CHS promoter activation in parsley
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CPRF-1 is one of three parsley bZIP factors binding a functional cis-element in the CHS promoter. 2. Its transcript is induced when dark-grown parsley cells are exposed to UV light. 3. It is implicated in light-responsive control of flavonoid biosynthesis.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Induced by UV exposure in dark-grown parsley cells
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Links UV/light signaling to CHS activation and flavonoid accumulation.

Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: binds functional light-responsive cis-element in CHS promoter
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: CPRF-2 is a UV-induced bZIP factor associated with light-regulated CHS transcription
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CPRF-2 binds the functional light-responsive cis-element in the parsley CHS promoter. 2. Its transcript is induced by UV exposure. 3. It likely participates in activation of flavonoid biosynthesis under light stress.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Induced by UV exposure in dark-grown parsley cells
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Participates in UV-responsive activation of flavonoid accumulation.

Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: binds functional light-responsive cis-element in CHS promoter
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: CPRF-3 is a UV-induced bZIP factor associated with CHS transcriptional activation
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CPRF-3 is part of the parsley CPRF bZIP family that binds a light-responsive element in the CHS promoter. 2. Its transcript increases after UV exposure. 3. It likely contributes to induction of flavonoid biosynthesis under light stress.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Induced by UV exposure in dark-grown parsley cells
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Participates in UV-responsive flavonoid pathway activation.

Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: binds H-box and G-box elements; likely translocates from cytosol to nucleus on elicitation
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: CHS15 promoter and other H-box/G-box-containing genes
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: KAP-1 is an H-box/G-box-binding factor implicated in stress and developmental regulation of bean CHS15
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: classical biochemical purification; DNA-binding assays; subcellular localization evidence
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. KAP-1 binds the H-box implicated in bean CHS15 stress/developmental expression. 2. It also binds the G-box with high affinity. 3. Preliminary evidence suggests cytosolic localization and elicitation-induced nuclear translocation.
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: DNA-binding factor; exact family not defined
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Represents a point of convergence between stress and developmental control of flavonoid biosynthesis.

Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: KAP-2 is an H-box-binding factor implicated in regulation of bean CHS15 expression
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. KAP-2 was isolated as a factor binding the H-box of the bean CHS15 promoter. 2. The H-box is implicated in both stress and developmental regulation. 3. KAP-2 likely participates with other factors in coordinating CHS expression.
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: DNA-binding factor; exact family not defined
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Supports stimulus-responsive flavonoid pathway gene expression.
